quiz_question: Which whale species is distinctive for having unusually long, knobby pectoral fins?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, humpback whales have a distinctive body shape with long pectoral fins and tubercles on the head. Their genus name Megaptera literally means "large-winged" in Greek, referring to these remarkable flippers.

quiz_question: Which is the only animal species where the male carries the pregnancy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, seahorses are unique in the animal kingdom — the male carries eggs in a specialized brood pouch and gives birth to live young. This role reversal is found in no other vertebrate species.

quiz_question: What unique swimming posture makes seahorses unlike any other fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, seahorses feature an upright posture and a curled prehensile tail. They are the only fish to swim vertically, using their dorsal fin for propulsion. Their prehensile tail anchors them to coral or seagrass when resting.

quiz_question: In which country were goldfish first selectively bred for color over 1,000 years ago?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the goldfish is native to China and was first selectively bred for color in imperial China more than 1,000 years ago, where several distinct breeds were developed. It is one of humanity's oldest ornamental breeding projects.

quiz_question: Which living land animal is the closest relative of whales and dolphins?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, genetic and fossil evidence places cetaceans (whales, dolphins, porpoises) within the terrestrial even-toed ungulates, most closely related to the hippopotamids. The shared ancestor walked on land about 50 million years ago.

quiz_question: How many living cetacean species exist, divided between toothed and baleen whales?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are approximately 90 living cetacean species split into two parvorders: Odontoceti (75 toothed whale species including dolphins and porpoises) and Mysticeti (15 baleen whale species including blue and humpback whales).

quiz_question: Which marine mammal relies on fur rather than blubber to stay warm?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the sea otter has the densest coat of fur in the animal kingdom. Unlike most marine mammals that rely on blubber, sea otters use their exceptionally thick fur to stay warm in cold Pacific waters.

quiz_question: Sea otters are one of the few non-primates to use what as tools?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sea otters use rocks to dislodge prey and open shells, making them one of the few mammal species known to use tools. They often float on their backs and hammer shellfish against a rock balanced on their chest.

quiz_question: How do sea otters protect kelp forests from destruction?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sea otters control sea urchin populations which would otherwise inflict extensive damage to kelp forest ecosystems. This makes them a keystone species — their presence or absence determines the health of an entire ecosystem.

quiz_question: After the fur trade, how many sea otters remained worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sea otters were hunted extensively for their fur between 1741 and 1911. The world population collapsed from an estimated 150,000-300,000 to just 1,000-2,000 individuals. An international hunting ban and conservation programs allowed the species to recover.

quiz_question: What is the maximum recorded length of a northern pike?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, northern pike have a maximum recorded length of up to 150 cm and maximum weights of 28.4 kg. They grow larger in Eurasia than in North America, particularly in coastal Eurasian regions.

quiz_question: What share of all living vertebrate species on Earth are ray-finned fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Actinopterygii (ray-finned fish) constitute nearly 99% of the over 30,000 living fish species and, by species count, comprise over 50% of all living vertebrates — more than all mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians combined.

quiz_question: How deep can a swordfish exceptionally dive below the ocean surface?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, swordfish can typically be found from near the surface to a depth of 550 m, and can exceptionally dive to 2,878 m (9,442 ft). Their highly migratory nature takes them across tropical and temperate parts of all three major oceans.

quiz_question: Where did the guppy (the world's most widely distributed tropical fish) originally come from?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, guppies originate from northeast South America but have been introduced worldwide. Wikidata confirms they are invasive in Australia and Madagascar. They are highly adaptable and used as model organisms in ecology and evolution research.

quiz_question: Which living fish group is the closest relative to all amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lungfish represent the closest living relatives of the tetrapods, which includes all living amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. Their fossil record extends over 410 million years into the Early Devonian, and today only six species survive.

quiz_question: How many of the seven sea turtle species are currently threatened with extinction?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, five of the seven existing sea turtle species are listed as threatened with extinction globally on the IUCN Red List. The remaining two are not currently considered threatened. Sea turtles have existed for over 110 million years.

quiz_question: How many million years ago did sea turtles first appear on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the start time for Chelonioidea (sea turtles) is approximately 110 million years ago. They predate the end-Cretaceous mass extinction by tens of millions of years, making them one of Earth's most ancient surviving reptile groups.

quiz_question: Why was the nutria (coypu) introduced to Europe, eventually becoming an invasive species?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the nutria was introduced to North America, Europe, and Asia primarily by fur farmers. Although still hunted for fur in some regions, its destructive burrowing and feeding habits make it an invasive species in several countries, including across the European Union per Wikidata.

quiz_question: How much did the IGFA all-tackle world-record northern pike weigh?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the IGFA all-tackle world-record northern pike was caught by Lothar Louis on Greffern Lake, Germany, on 16 October 1986, weighing 25 kg (55 lb). Northern pike grow larger in Eurasia than in North America.

quiz_question: What term describes the northern pike's distribution across the entire Northern Hemisphere?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, northern pike have a holarctic distribution — meaning they are found in fresh waters of the entire Northern Hemisphere, spanning North America, Europe, and Asia.

quiz_question: What exceptional depth have swordfish been recorded reaching?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, swordfish are typically found from near the surface to 550 m depth, but have been exceptionally recorded at depths up to 2,878 m (9,442 ft). They are the sole member of family Xiphiidae.

quiz_question: What do swordfish lose completely as they reach adulthood?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, swordfish are elongated and round-bodied and lose all teeth and scales by adulthood. Despite this, they are powerful predators that commonly reach 3 m (10 ft) in length and up to 650 kg in weight.

quiz_question: Where did the guppy fish originally come from before spreading worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, guppies originate from northeast South America but have been introduced to many environments worldwide. They are now one of the most popular freshwater aquarium fish species, and Wikidata lists them as invasive in Australia and Madagascar.

quiz_question: How do guppies reproduce differently from most fish?
correct_answer: They give birth to live young
distractors: They carry eggs in their mouths
They lay eggs in bubble nests
explanation: According to Wikipedia, guppies are considered livebearers — members of family Poeciliidae. Male guppies are smaller than females and have ornamental caudal and dorsal fins, while females carry developing young internally until birth.

quiz_question: How long is the gestation period of a nutria (coypu)?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the nutria (Myocastor coypus) has a gestation period of 133 days. Originally from South America, it was introduced to Europe and North America primarily by fur farmers and became an invasive species.

quiz_question: Why were nutria originally brought to Europe and North America?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nutria were introduced to North America, Europe, and Asia primarily by fur farmers. Their destructive burrowing and feeding habits have since made them an invasive pest in several countries including the European Union.

quiz_question: What unusual daytime social behavior do some hammerhead sharks show?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, unlike most sharks, some hammerhead species will congregate and swim in large schools during the day, becoming solitary hunters at night. This daytime schooling behavior is exceptionally unusual among sharks.

quiz_question: What vision advantage does the hammerhead shark's wide, flat head provide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cephalofoil (T-shaped head) gives hammerhead sharks superior binocular vision and depth perception, plus increased surface area for electroreceptors. Multiple functions have been proposed for the cephalofoil including sensory reception, maneuvering, and prey manipulation.

quiz_question: How much can an adult ocean sunfish (Mola mola) weigh?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, adult ocean sunfish typically weigh between 247 and 1,000 kg (545-2,205 lb). The fish resembles a head without a tail and can be as tall as it is long when fins are extended, reaching about 175 cm (6 ft).

quiz_question: What does the European Union ban regarding the ocean sunfish's family?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, EU regulations ban the sale of fish and fishery products derived from the family Molidae. Despite this, sunfish are considered a delicacy in Japan, Korea, and Taiwan.

quiz_question: Which fish group are the closest living relatives of all land vertebrates, including humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lungfish (dipnoans) represent the closest living relatives of the tetrapods — which includes all living amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. Their fossil record extends over 410 million years.

quiz_question: How many species of lungfish are alive today?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, today there are only six known species of lungfish, living in Africa, South America, and Australia. They were formerly globally distributed, and their fossil record extends over 410 million years into the Early Devonian.

quiz_question: Which shark species has the widest food spectrum of all sharks?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tiger shark has the widest food spectrum of all sharks, eating crustaceans, fish, seals, birds, squid, sea turtles, sea snakes, dolphins, and even inedible man-made objects. It has been called a 'garbage eater' and has only one recorded natural predator: the orca.

quiz_question: What happens to a tiger shark's distinctive stripes as it grows into an adult?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tiger shark's name derives from the dark stripes down its body, which resemble a tiger's pattern. These stripes are prominent in young sharks but fade as the shark matures, meaning adults look noticeably different from juveniles.

quiz_question: What is the tiger shark's IUCN conservation status?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the tiger shark holds an IUCN status of Near Threatened, primarily due to finning and human fishing. Despite its reputation as one of the most dangerous sharks to humans, it is humans who threaten the tiger shark's survival.

quiz_question: Why were Siamese fighting fish originally domesticated in Thailand over 1,000 years ago?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Siamese fighting fish were domesticated at least 1,000 years ago in Thailand — among the earliest domesticated fish in history. They were bred for aggression and used in gambling matches akin to cockfighting, not for their decorative appearance.

quiz_question: What special organ lets Siamese fighting fish breathe air at the water's surface?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Siamese fighting fish possess a labyrinth organ — unique to the suborder Anabantoidei — that allows intake of atmospheric oxygen from the surface. This is why bettas can tolerate low oxygen levels and poor water quality in rice paddies and stagnant pools.

quiz_question: What is the IUCN conservation status of the wild Siamese fighting fish?
explanation: According to Wikidata, wild Betta splendens is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, threatened by increasing pollution and habitat destruction. This is a stark contrast to their enormous abundance in captivity — the Siamese fighting fish is also the national aquatic animal of Thailand.

quiz_question: Which animal is the only known living creature with a skull but no vertebral column?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hagfish are the only known living animals that have a skull but lack a vertebral column, though they do have rudimentary vertebrae. This makes them a profound evolutionary outlier among vertebrate animals.

quiz_question: What unique defense does the hagfish use that can suffocate attacking fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hagfish release copious amounts of slime from mucous glands in their skin when threatened. The slime is so thick and viscous it can clog the gills of attacking fish, effectively suffocating them — a remarkably effective defense for a jawless deep-sea scavenger.

quiz_question: How many millions of years ago did the earliest stem group hagfish appear in the fossil record?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the oldest-known stem group hagfish are from the Late Carboniferous, around 310 million years ago. Modern hagfish first appeared in the mid-Cretaceous around 100 million years ago. This makes hagfish one of Earth's longest-surviving animal lineages — they watched the dinosaurs come and go.

quiz_question: What Italian restaurant name is the European seabass widely sold under across North America?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the European seabass (Dicentrarchus labrax) is widely known in North America by one of its Italian names, branzino. It is the most important commercially cultured fish in the Mediterranean, though its IUCN status is Near Threatened due to a declining wild population.

quiz_question: What fraction of all living vertebrates are ray-finned fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Actinopterygii (ray-finned fish) comprise over 50% of all living vertebrate species — more than all mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians combined. They dominate the subphylum Vertebrata by species count.

quiz_question: How heavy can the giant sunfish, a ray-finned fish, grow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, extant ray-finned fish species span from the 8 mm Paedocypris to the giant sunfish at 2,700 kg. The extinct Leedsichthys from the Jurassic is estimated to have grown to 16.5 m, the largest ray-finned fish ever known.

quiz_question: Which fish group gave rise to all land vertebrates, including humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Osteichthyes divides into ray-finned fish and lobe-finned fish (Sarcopterygii). The lobe-finned branch gave rise to all tetrapods — amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals. In a cladistic sense, humans are technically bony fish.

quiz_question: How many living species of coelacanth exist today?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lobe-finned fish (Sarcopterygii) were once dominant freshwater predators during the Carboniferous and Permian periods but suffered massive decline after the Permian-Triassic extinction. Today only 2 coelacanth species and 6 lungfish species survive as the non-tetrapod remnants of this lineage.

quiz_question: How many million years ago did the jawless fish lineage first appear in fossils?
explanation: According to Wikidata (start time: -535000000), jawless fish (Agnatha) first appeared in the Cambrian. Wikipedia confirms the oldest fossil agnathans appeared in the Cambrian, making them among the most ancient vertebrate groups still represented by living species — hagfishes and lampreys.

quiz_question: How many million years ago did horseshoe crabs first appear in the fossil record?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Xiphosura first appear in the fossil record around 445 million years ago in the Early Ordovician. Wikipedia confirms the group has hardly changed in appearance in hundreds of millions of years — modern horseshoe crabs look almost identical to prehistoric genera.

quiz_question: What weapon takes its name from the electric ray genus Torpedo?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, electric rays of the genus Torpedo gave their name to the torpedo weapon. The Latin torpere means to be stiffened or paralyzed — exactly what happens when someone touches these fish, which can produce 8 to 220 volts depending on species.

quiz_question: Which rare trait do tuna share with only opah and mackerel sharks among all fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tuna, opah, and mackerel sharks are the only fish species that can maintain a body temperature higher than the surrounding water. This partial endothermy helps them sustain high speeds — yellowfin tuna can reach 75 km/h.

quiz_question: Why is the Hawaiian name mahi-mahi used instead of dolphin fish in markets?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, mahi-mahi (Coryphaena hippurus) is widely called dolphin as a common name — but it is a ray-finned fish completely unrelated to the marine mammal dolphin. The Hawaiian name mahi-mahi is used specifically to avoid this confusion in markets.

quiz_question: What is the typical body length of an individual bryozoan animal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, individual bryozoan zooids are typically about 0.5 millimetres long and use a crown of tentacles called a lophophore for filter feeding. Despite their tiny size, 5,869 living species exist and their colonies can take dramatic forms including fans, bushes, and encrusting sheets.

quiz_question: What is the IUCN conservation status of the European seabass?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the European seabass (Dicentrarchus labrax) has IUCN conservation status Near Threatened due to a decreasing population. Wikipedia confirms it is the most important fish currently cultured in the Mediterranean, and the exclusive sea bass sold in UK and Irish restaurants.

quiz_question: How many million years ago did bichirs diverge from other ray-finned fish?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bichirs (Polypteridae) are considered the sister group to all other extant ray-finned fishes (Actinopteri) and likely diverged at least 330 million years ago. The oldest bichir fossils are around 100 million years old from the early Late Cretaceous of South America and Africa.

quiz_question: How many genera are in the barracuda family Sphyraenidae?
explanation: According to Wikidata, barracudas (Sphyraena) have a fossil record going back about 56 million years. According to Wikipedia, the family Sphyraenidae was named by Constantine Samuel Rafinesque in 1815, and Sphyraena is the only genus in the family.

quiz_question: What is the maximum voltage an electric ray can discharge?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, electric rays (order Torpediniformes) produce electric discharges ranging from 8 to 220 volts depending on species. The torpedo undersea weapon was named after the genus Torpedo — from the Latin torpere meaning 'to be stiffened or paralyzed', describing what happens when you touch one.

quiz_question: What does the Latin root of the word 'torpedo' mean?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name 'torpedo' comes from the Latin torpere meaning 'to be stiffened or paralyzed', describing the numbing effect of an electric ray's shock. The naval weapon was named after the fish — not the other way around.

quiz_question: How long ago did horseshoe crabs first appear in the fossil record?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Xiphosura (horseshoe crabs) first appear in the fossil record in the Early Ordovician, around 480 million years ago. The group has hardly changed in appearance since, making modern horseshoe crabs look almost identical to prehistoric genera — they are considered true living fossils.

quiz_question: Which turtle is the largest freshwater turtle in North America?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the alligator snapping turtle (Macrochelys temminckii) is the largest freshwater turtle in North America and one of the heaviest living freshwater turtles in the world. It is endemic to freshwater habitats in the United States and is currently listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List.

quiz_question: How long can common snapping turtles live in the wild according to Algonquin Park data?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, long-term mark-recapture data from Algonquin Park in Ontario, Canada suggest a maximum wild age of over 100 years for common snapping turtles. Northern populations also mature later — at 15 to 20 years — compared to about 12 years in southern populations.

quiz_question: How fast can the fastest-growing kelp species grow per day?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the kelp genera Macrocystis and Nereocystis can grow as fast as half a metre (about 20 inches) per day, ultimately reaching 30 to 80 metres tall. Despite resembling plants and using photosynthesis, kelp is not a plant — it is a stramenopile.

quiz_question: What rank do nurse sharks hold in documented shark bites on humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nurse sharks are ranked fourth in documented shark bites on humans — surprising given their calm, sedentary nature. The bites likely result from incautious behavior by divers who approach them precisely because of that calmness. They are robust enough to tolerate capture and tagging, making them important research subjects.

quiz_question: Which ocean fish is also called 'dolphinfish' despite being completely unrelated to dolphins?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the mahi-mahi (Coryphaena hippurus) is widely called both 'dolphin' and 'dorado', creating two sources of confusion: it is not the aquatic mammal dolphin, and it is not Salminus brasiliensis, the freshwater fish also known as dorado. It is a surface-dwelling fish found in offshore temperate, tropical, and subtropical waters worldwide.

quiz_question: How long ago did bichirs diverge from all other ray-finned fishes?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bichirs (family Polypteridae) likely diverged from Actinopteri (other ray-finned fishes) at least 330 million years ago — predating the dinosaurs. They are considered the sister group to all other extant ray-finned fishes. All species live in freshwater habitats in tropical Africa and the Nile River system.

quiz_question: What percentage of the world's ilish fish supply comes from Bangladesh as of 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2023, 97% of the world's total ilish supply originates in Bangladesh. The fish contributes about 12% of total fish production and about 1.15% of GDP in Bangladesh. About 450,000 people are directly involved in catching it, with four to five million indirectly involved in the trade.

quiz_question: What cognitive test can manta rays pass that most animals fail?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, manta rays have one of the highest brain-to-body ratios of all fish and can pass the mirror test — a benchmark of self-awareness typically only demonstrated by great apes, dolphins, and elephants. This places them in elite cognitive company among all vertebrates.

quiz_question: How wide can the largest manta ray species grow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mobula birostris is the largest of three known manta ray species, reaching 7 m (23 ft) in width. The smallest species, M. alfredi, reaches only 5.5 m. All three species have triangular pectoral fins, horn-shaped cephalic fins, and large forward-facing mouths.

quiz_question: What body part of manta rays is harvested for traditional Chinese medicine, threatening them?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, manta rays face anthropogenic threats including pollution, entanglement in fishing nets, and direct harvesting of their gill rakers for use in Chinese medicine. Both M. birostris and M. alfredi are listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, and their slow reproductive rate exacerbates these threats.

quiz_question: What is the only dinosaur lineage known to have survived the extinction event 66 million years ago?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the fossil record shows that birds are feathered dinosaurs, having evolved from earlier theropods during the Late Jurassic epoch. They are the only dinosaur lineage known to have survived the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event approximately 66 million years ago.

quiz_question: How long were the largest sauropod dinosaurs, the biggest land animals ever?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the largest sauropod dinosaurs are estimated to have reached lengths of 39.7 meters (130 feet) and heights of 18 m (59 ft), making them the largest land animals of all time. Despite their colossal size, they were herbivores.

quiz_question: How many distinct genera of non-avian dinosaurs have paleontologists identified from fossils?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, using fossil evidence, paleontologists have identified over 900 distinct genera and more than 1,000 different species of non-avian dinosaurs. The name 'dinosaur' (meaning 'terrible lizard') was coined by Sir Richard Owen in 1842.

quiz_question: Why do chameleons primarily change colour, contrary to popular belief?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, chameleons are best known for colour-shifting, which is widely believed to be camouflage — but the primary function is social signalling and mood expression. Different species shift in different ways, from simple brightness shifts (browns) to vivid colour-combinations (reds, yellows, greens, blues).

quiz_question: How do chameleon eyes move differently from most other vertebrates?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, chameleons' eyes are independently mobile, and their brain constantly analyses two separate, individual images of their environment simultaneously. Only when hunting prey do the eyes focus forward in coordination, providing binocular stereoscopic vision for precise tongue strikes.

quiz_question: What percentage of all living amphibian species are frogs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, frogs account for around 88% of extant amphibian species and are one of the five most diverse vertebrate orders. The oldest fossil proto-frog, Triadobatrachus, is known from 250 million years ago in Madagascar.

quiz_question: Besides their lungs, through what do adult frogs also exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, frog skin is semi-permeable, making frogs susceptible to dehydration but enabling direct gas exchange across the skin. Adult frogs use a hybrid system: lungs aided by buccal pumping and direct cutaneous gas exchange — which is also why water pollution is so deadly to them.

quiz_question: What maximum age can wild common snapping turtles reach, according to Canadian field data?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lifespan in the wild is poorly known, but long-term mark-recapture data from Algonquin Park in Ontario, Canada suggest a maximum age over 100 years. Females in more northern populations also mature much later — at 15–20 years versus about 12 in southern populations.

quiz_question: Where do nurse sharks rank among shark species in documented bites on humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nurse sharks are considered inoffensive due to their calm, sedentary nature, yet they are ranked fourth in documented shark bites on humans. This is likely because divers behave incautiously around them, assuming they pose no threat.

quiz_question: Which title does the alligator snapping turtle hold in North America?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the alligator snapping turtle (Macrochelys temminckii) is the largest freshwater turtle in North America and one of the heaviest living freshwater turtles in the world. It is endemic to freshwater habitats in the United States and is listed as Endangered by the IUCN.

quiz_question: How far back does the barracuda genus Sphyraena trace in the fossil record?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the barracuda genus Sphyraena has a start time of approximately 56 million years ago. According to Wikipedia, barracudas are found in tropical and subtropical oceans worldwide, from the Atlantic to the Red Sea, Caribbean, and Pacific, residing near the top of the water and near coral reefs and sea grasses.

quiz_question: How many of the eight pangolin species are critically endangered?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of January 2020, three pangolin species (Manis culionensis, M. pentadactyla, and M. javanica) are critically endangered, three are endangered, and two are vulnerable on the IUCN Red List — meaning every single pangolin species is threatened.

quiz_question: How was a potential new pangolin species first identified in 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in September 2023 a ninth pangolin species was reported, but it is only known from confiscated scales — it has never been observed alive in the wild, making it one of the most mysterious mammals ever identified.

quiz_question: Why are pigs commonly used in human medical research?
correct_answer: Because they are biologically similar to humans
explanation: According to Wikipedia, pigs are biologically similar to humans and are thus frequently used for human medical research. This biological closeness makes them valuable models for studying human diseases, organ transplants, and surgical techniques.

quiz_question: What unexpected cognitive trait makes pigs stand out among farm animals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, pigs live in complex social groups and are considered one of the more intelligent mammals, reflected in their remarkable ability to learn. Studies have shown pigs can navigate mazes, recognize themselves, and respond to training.

quiz_question: Which animal made the ancient Silk Road trade route possible across inner Asia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bactrian camel's tolerance for cold, drought, and high altitudes enabled the travel of caravans on the Silk Road across the harsh steppes and mountains of inner Asia. Domesticated Bactrian camels have served as pack animals in this region since ancient times.

quiz_question: How long ago did domestic and wild Bactrian camels diverge as separate species?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, domestic Bactrian camels (Camelus bactrianus) do not descend from wild Bactrian camels (Camelus ferus), with the two species having split around 1 million years ago. This makes them separately evolved species, not just a domesticated version of the wild one.

quiz_question: How many Bactrian camels exist in the world, mostly in domesticated form?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Bactrian camel's population of 2 million exists mainly in the domesticated form. By contrast, wild Bactrian camels (a separate species) are critically endangered with only a few hundred remaining.

quiz_question: What lives inside sloth fur that both camouflages and nourishes the animal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sloth fur has grooved hairs that are host to symbiotic green algae. This algae camouflages the animal in the trees and also provides it nutrients — a remarkable two-way partnership between a mammal and a plant-like organism.

quiz_question: What tiny creature spends its entire life cycle living exclusively on a sloth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the algae in sloth fur also nourishes sloth moths — some species of which exist solely on sloths. These moths have evolved to live their entire lives on a single host animal, a remarkable example of host-specific symbiosis.

quiz_question: Why did sloths evolve to move so slowly?
distractors: To allow digestion of toxic leaves
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sloths' slowness permits their low-energy diet of leaves and avoids detection by predatory hawks and cats that hunt by sight. Moving so little they are essentially invisible to motion-detecting predators is a brilliant survival strategy.

quiz_question: How large did the biggest ancient ground sloths grow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, ground sloths ranging up to the size of elephants (like Megatherium) inhabited both North and South America during the Pleistocene Epoch. They became extinct during the Quaternary extinction event around 12,000 years ago.

quiz_question: From which wild bird was the modern chicken domesticated?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the chicken (Gallus gallus domesticus) is a domesticated form of the red junglefowl (Gallus gallus), originally native to Southeast Asia. It was first domesticated around 8,000 years ago.

quiz_question: How many chickens exist globally as of 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as of 2023 the global chicken population exceeds 26.5 billion, with more than 50 billion birds produced annually for consumption. This makes chickens by far the most numerous bird species on Earth.

quiz_question: How many eggs can a specially bred laying hen produce per year?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a hen bred for laying can produce over 300 eggs per year. This is the result of selective breeding over many generations — wild junglefowl, by comparison, lay far fewer eggs annually.

quiz_question: How many species of eagles exist worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are 68 species of eagles, and most are from Eurasia and Africa. Outside this area, just 14 species can be found — two in North America, nine in Central and South America, and three in Australia.

quiz_question: What does the term 'eagle' mean in formal scientific taxonomy?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the term 'eagle' generally carries no taxonomic weight. Only the subfamily Aquilinae comprises 'true eagles' at a genetic level — but many unrelated large raptors, including the bald eagle, are commonly called eagles regardless of their actual classification.

quiz_question: What is the top burst speed of the fastest bipedal land animal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common ostrich can run at 55 km/h sustained with short bursts up to about 70 km/h, which is the fastest land speed of any bipedal animal. This makes the world's largest bird also the fastest two-legged runner.

quiz_question: Why can the common ostrich be accurately called the world's largest living dinosaur?
distractors: It descends directly from T. rex
It has three toes like ancient raptors
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common ostrich is the largest living species of bird, and thus the largest living dinosaur — because birds evolved from theropod dinosaurs and are themselves classified as dinosaurs in modern cladistic taxonomy.

quiz_question: Which living bird lays the largest eggs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the ostrich lays the largest eggs of any living bird, and according to Wikidata, incubation takes 42 days. The extinct giant elephant bird of Madagascar and the south island giant moa of New Zealand laid larger eggs, but among living species, the ostrich holds the record.

quiz_question: How much can a common ostrich weigh at its heaviest?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the common ostrich can weigh up to 115 kg. Combined with its height of up to 2.8 m, it is the world's largest and heaviest bird — despite being completely flightless.

quiz_question: What is the typical mating pattern of swans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, swans usually mate for life, though separation sometimes occurs following nesting failure. If a mate dies, the surviving swan will typically take up with another. They are among the most well-known examples of avian monogamy.

quiz_question: How many eggs can a swan lay in a single clutch?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the number of eggs in each swan clutch ranges from three to eight. As the largest waterfowl, swans invest significant energy in raising their cygnets through a relatively long incubation period.

quiz_question: Among waterfowl, what size distinction do swans hold?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, swans are the largest waterfowl and are often among the largest flighted birds within their geographic range. They belong to the tribe Cygnini within the subfamily Anserinae, sharing close relatives with geese and ducks.

quiz_question: How tall is the average adult emperor penguin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the emperor penguin (Aptenodytes forsteri) is the largest living penguin species: adults average about 1.1 m (3 ft 7 in) tall and weigh 35 kg (77 lb). This makes it not only the largest penguin but one of the largest flightless birds.

quiz_question: How did some prehistoric penguins compare in size to adult humans?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, some prehistoric penguin species were enormous — as tall or as heavy as an adult human. At least one giant species lived in a region around 2,000 km south of the equator 35 million years ago, during the Late Eocene, when the climate was decidedly warmer than today.

quiz_question: Which is the only penguin species found north of the equator?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, penguins live almost exclusively in the Southern Hemisphere. The single exception is the Galapagos penguin, which lives at and slightly north of the equator, making it the only penguin species to venture into the Northern Hemisphere.

quiz_question: What remarkable ability beyond tool use was discovered in crows in 2002?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2002 research found some crow species capable of not only tool use but also tool construction. Crows are now considered among the world's most intelligent animals, with an encephalization quotient equal to that of many non-human primates.

quiz_question: What is the collective noun for a group of crows?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the collective name for a group of crows is a 'flock' or a 'murder'. The term 'murder' has been associated with crows for centuries, likely because of their dark plumage, their habit of congregating around carrion, and their eerie calls.

quiz_question: Where did the original corvid stock that gave rise to crows and ravens first evolve?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, members of the genus Corvus appear to have evolved in Asia from corvid stock that had first evolved in Australia. This means today's widespread crows and ravens trace their ultimate evolutionary roots to the Australian continent.

quiz_question: Which bird holds the title of most widely distributed wild bird?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, intentional and accidental introductions of the house sparrow to regions including Australasia, Africa, and the Americas have made it the most widely distributed wild bird on Earth. It is native to Europe, the Mediterranean Basin, and much of Asia.

quiz_question: What unusual cultural symbolism has the house sparrow historically represented?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of its numbers, ubiquity, and association with human settlements, the house sparrow has historically been a cultural symbol of lust, sexual potency, commonness, and vulgarity.

quiz_question: How long can a common raven live in the wild?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, although their typical lifespan is considerably shorter, common ravens can live more than 23 years in the wild. Young ravens travel in flocks but later mate for life, with each mated pair defending a territory.

quiz_question: Which bird is revered as a spiritual or godlike figure across Scandinavia, North America, and Siberia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common raven has been revered as a spiritual figure or godlike creature in Scandinavia, ancient Ireland and Wales, Bhutan, the northwest coast of North America, and Siberia and northeast Asia. This cultural prominence spans multiple continents and millennia.

quiz_question: What cognitive test can the Eurasian magpie pass that few non-mammal species can?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Eurasian magpie is one of the few bird species known to pass the mirror test, which assesses whether an animal recognises its own reflection. Wikidata lists 'self-awareness' as a documented capability. Its nidopallium expansion is comparable in relative size to that of chimpanzees, gorillas, orangutans, and humans.

quiz_question: Whose brain size does the Eurasian magpie's nidopallium match in relative terms?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the expansion of the magpie's nidopallium (a region of the avian forebrain) is approximately the same in relative size as the brains of chimpanzees, gorillas, orangutans, and humans. This structural similarity is linked to the magpie's remarkable intelligence.

quiz_question: Which wild duck species is the main ancestor of most domestic duck breeds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the mallard is the main ancestor of most breeds of domestic duck. Its naturally evolved wild gene pool has been genetically polluted by domestic and feral mallard populations, and the species interbreeds with indigenous wild ducks in many regions.

quiz_question: What swimming ability do mallard ducklings have immediately after hatching?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, mallard ducklings are precocial and fully capable of swimming as soon as they hatch. Egg incubation takes 27 to 28 days, and fledging takes 50 to 60 days after hatching.

quiz_question: How do mallards threaten some native wild duck populations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the non-migratory mallard interbreeds with indigenous wild ducks of closely related species through genetic pollution by producing fertile offspring. Complete hybridisation of various species of wild duck gene pools could result in the extinction of many indigenous waterfowl.

quiz_question: Why are abandoned woodpecker holes important to other birds?
distractors: They create shade for ground nesters
explanation: According to Wikipedia, woodpeckers usually nest and roost in holes they excavate in tree trunks. Their abandoned holes are of importance to other cavity-nesting birds. Many species — from owls to small ducks — depend entirely on abandoned woodpecker holes, as they cannot excavate their own.

quiz_question: Which major landmass, famously full of unique wildlife, has no native woodpeckers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, members of the woodpecker family Picidae are found worldwide except for Australia, New Guinea, New Zealand, Madagascar, and the extreme polar regions. The family includes about 240 species in 35 genera, yet none colonised the Australian region.

quiz_question: What hunting behaviour do Komodo dragons exhibit that is exceptional among reptiles?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Komodo dragons are apex predators due to their exceptional size and group hunting behaviour — both being extraordinary among reptiles. They hunt and ambush prey including invertebrates, birds, and mammals, occasionally even attacking humans.

quiz_question: Where is the tuatara's mysterious third eye located?
distractors: At the base of its skull
At the tip of its snout
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tuatara has a number of unusual features in its skeleton compared to lizards, and is able to hear although no external ear is present. It possesses a parietal eye (third eye) on the top of its head — visible in hatchlings but soon covered by scales. Its function may relate to light sensing for circadian rhythm regulation.

quiz_question: What makes the common adder extraordinary in terms of geographic range among terrestrial snakes?
correct_answer: It has the widest range of any terrestrial snake
distractors: It lives at the highest altitude
It covers the most latitudes north
It lives on the most continents
It tolerates the widest range of prey
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common adder (Vipera berus) is extremely widespread and can be found throughout much of Europe and as far east as East Asia including the Pacific coast. Wikidata records its range across 26 countries including UK, Russia, China, and Mongolia — spanning the entire Eurasian landmass.

quiz_question: Until 1938, scientists believed the coelacanth was what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, only two species of coelacanths survive today as non-tetrapod sarcopterygians (lobe-finned fish). Coelacanths were known only from fossils and believed long extinct until a living specimen was found off South Africa in 1938, stunning the scientific world.

quiz_question: How many species of lizards exist worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lizard is the common name for all squamate reptiles other than snakes (and to a lesser extent amphisbaenians), encompassing over 7,000 species ranging across all continents except Antarctica. They range in size from tiny geckos a few centimetres long to the 3-metre Komodo dragon.

quiz_question: How does the crocodile's cold sensitivity compare to that of alligators?
correct_answer: Crocodiles are much more sensitive to cold
distractors: Alligators are more sensitive to cold
explanation: According to Wikipedia, all crocodiles are tropical species that, unlike alligators, are very sensitive to cold. This restricts them to warm tropical regions, while alligators can survive in more temperate climates such as the southeastern United States and eastern China.

quiz_question: Where does the common quail spend its winters?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common quail is mainly migratory, breeding in the western Palearctic (Europe and western Asia) and wintering in Africa and southern India. It is widespread in Europe and North Africa and is categorised by the IUCN as Least Concern.

quiz_question: How many recognised species are in the heron family Ardeidae?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the family Ardeidae contains 75 recognised species. These include birds referred to as herons, egrets, and bitterns — all members of the same biological family despite their different common names.

quiz_question: What do herons use to clean fish slime from their feathers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, herons are among the bird groups that have powder down — specialised feathers that crumble into a fine powder. This powder is used to clean fish slime and oil off their plumage, much like a dry shampoo.

quiz_question: How much does a European robin weigh, per Wikidata?
explanation: Wikidata records the European robin's mass as 2.4 grams. A US nickel weighs 5 grams — meaning this familiar garden bird weighs less than half a coin, yet it navigates and migrates across continents.

quiz_question: How do male and female European robins differ in appearance?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the male and female European robin are identical in plumage — both have the iconic orange-red breast and face lined with grey. This is unusual among songbirds, where males are often more brightly coloured. Juveniles, however, are distinctly brown and freckled.

quiz_question: Which stork ties with the Andean condor for the widest wingspan of any living land bird?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the marabou stork has a wingspan of 3.2 m (10 ft 6 in) and weight up to 8 kg, and along with the Andean condor holds the record for the widest wingspan of all living land birds.

quiz_question: How deep can a stork nest grow after years of reuse?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, stork nests are often very large and may be used for many years. Some nests grow to over 2 metres in diameter and about 3 metres in depth — roughly the height of a standard room ceiling.

quiz_question: Which birds are pelicans most closely related to, surprising scientists?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, pelicans were long thought to be related to frigatebirds, cormorants, tropicbirds, and gannets, but are now known to be most closely related to the shoebill and hamerkop — two distinctive African birds.

quiz_question: How far back does fossil evidence of pelicans date?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fossil evidence of pelicans dates back at least 36 million years, recovered from late Eocene strata of Egypt. Remarkably, the fossil bears striking similarity to modern pelicans, showing the bird family is essentially unchanged.

quiz_question: How many domestic pigeon breeds all descend from the wild rock dove?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the domestic pigeon includes about 1,000 different breeds, all descended from the rock dove (Columba livia). Escaped domestic pigeons are the origin of the feral pigeons found in cities around the world.

quiz_question: How many wild and feral rock doves are estimated to live worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the rock dove has an estimated population of 17 to 28 million wild and feral birds in Europe alone, and up to 120 million worldwide. This makes it one of the most numerous bird species on the planet.

quiz_question: What is the fastest recorded dive speed of a peregrine falcon?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, peregrine falcons have been recorded diving at speeds of 320 km/h (200 mph), with the fastest recorded dive reaching a vertical speed of 390 km/h (240 mph), making them the fastest-moving creatures on Earth.

quiz_question: In falcons, which sex is typically larger?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, falcons exhibit sexual dimorphism where females are typically larger than males. This allows a mated pair to exploit a wider range of prey species, since the two differently-sized birds can catch different sized animals.

quiz_question: Which famous writer noted the common starling's ability to mimic sounds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common starling's gift for mimicry has been noted in literature including the Mabinogion and the works of Pliny the Elder and William Shakespeare. Starlings can mimic other birds, animals, and even human speech.

quiz_question: Which region successfully prevented common starling colonisation through controls?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, culling and control programs have had limited success in most regions except in preventing the colonisation of Western Australia. The starling remains established across most of its introduced range worldwide.

quiz_question: Which country features the golden eagle as its official national symbol on its flag?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the golden eagle is listed as an official symbol of Mexico (it appears on the Mexican flag), and also represents Germany, Albania, Austria, Kazakhstan, Egypt, and the Roman legion, among others.

quiz_question: How large can a golden eagle's home territory be?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, golden eagles maintain home ranges or territories that may be as large as 200 km2 (77 sq mi). They build large nests in cliffs and high places, returning to the same nest for several breeding years.

quiz_question: What is the maximum wingspan of a female Eurasian eagle-owl?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, female Eurasian eagle-owls can grow to a total length of 75 cm with a wingspan of 188 cm (6 feet 2 inches). Males are slightly smaller. This makes the Eurasian eagle-owl one of the largest owls in the world.

quiz_question: How have tame Eurasian eagle-owls been used at airports?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tame eagle-owls have occasionally been used in pest control because of their size to deter large birds such as gulls from nesting at airports and buildings.

quiz_question: What does the osprey eat almost exclusively?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the osprey's diet consists almost exclusively of fish. This specialisation is reflected in its three historical common names — sea hawk, river hawk, and fish hawk — and in unique physical adaptations for catching prey underwater.

quiz_question: Why is the osprey placed in its own unique family, Pandionidae?
distractors: It is descended from ancient falcons
It is larger than all eagles
explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of its unique physical characteristics and unique hunting behaviour, the osprey is classified in its own taxonomic genus Pandion and its own family Pandionidae — meaning it has no close living relatives among birds of prey.

quiz_question: What makes the peregrine falcon's English and scientific names both fitting?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, both the English and scientific names of the peregrine (Falco peregrinus) mean 'wandering falcon', referring to the migratory habits of many northern populations. It is also the world's most widespread raptor.

quiz_question: Which pesticide nearly wiped out the peregrine falcon before being banned?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the peregrine falcon became endangered in many areas due to widespread use of various pesticides, especially DDT. Since the ban on DDT from the early 1970s, populations have recovered, supported by large-scale protection of nesting places and releases to the wild.

quiz_question: What prey species mainly sustains urban peregrine falcons in cities?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, feral pigeons support many peregrine populations as a staple food source, especially in urban settings, because of their greater abundance in cities than most other birds. Peregrines use tall buildings as substitute cliff nest sites.

quiz_question: How much does a common nightingale weigh according to Wikidata?
explanation: Wikidata records the common nightingale's mass at 2.65 grams. Despite being tiny — lighter than most coins — this small passerine produces one of the most celebrated songs in the natural world, sung powerfully both day and night.

quiz_question: Which nightingales produce the famous song — males, females, or both?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common nightingale is a small passerine best known for its powerful and beautiful song. The song is produced by unpaired males during breeding season, functioning to attract a mate — once paired, males largely stop singing at night.

quiz_question: How many days does it take for a nightingale's eggs to hatch?
explanation: Wikidata records the nightingale's egg incubation period as 14 days, with an average litter (clutch) size of 4.5 eggs. The species is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List.

quiz_question: What is the maximum wingspan of great albatrosses?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, great albatrosses are among the largest of flying birds, with wingspans reaching up to 2.5–3.5 metres (8.2–11.5 ft) and bodies over 1 metre in length. This enormous wingspan lets them soar using dynamic and slope soaring with minimal effort.

quiz_question: What is the name of the oldest-known wild bird in the world?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a Laysan albatross named Wisdom on Midway Island is the oldest-known wild bird in the world. She was first banded in 1956 by Chandler Robbins and continued raising chicks well into her 70s.

quiz_question: What is the greatest current threat to albatross survival?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, of the 22 albatross species recognised by the IUCN, 21 are listed at some level of concern. Longline fisheries pose the greatest threat: birds are attracted to the bait, become hooked on the lines, and drown.

quiz_question: Which US state claims a non-native bird, the ring-necked pheasant, as its state bird?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the ring-necked pheasant is the state bird of South Dakota, one of only two US state birds that is not a species native to the United States. The pheasant is native to Asia and was introduced globally as a game bird.

quiz_question: What is considered one of the world's most hunted game birds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common pheasant is one of the world's most hunted birds; it has been introduced for that purpose to many regions and is also common on game farms. It is perhaps the most widespread and ancient gamebird of more than regional importance in the whole world.

quiz_question: Which emu parent incubates the eggs and raises the chicks alone?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in emus the male does the incubation; during this process he hardly eats or drinks and loses a significant amount of weight. The young are nurtured by their fathers after hatching, reaching full size after around six months.

quiz_question: Which country lost an infamous 1932 military operation against a bird species?
explanation: Wikidata lists 'Emu War' as a significant event associated with the emu. In 1932, the Australian Army deployed soldiers with machine guns to cull emus damaging crops in Western Australia. The operation was largely unsuccessful, and troops withdrew — remembered as the 'Emu War'.

quiz_question: What percentage of a female kiwi's body weight can her egg reach?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, kiwi eggs are one of the largest in proportion to body size — up to 20% of the female's weight — of any order of bird in the world. This is equivalent to a human giving birth to a roughly 14 kg baby.

quiz_question: Where on the beak are a kiwi's nostrils, uniquely among birds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, kiwis use their nostrils at the end of their long beak to detect prey before they see it — a unique adaptation among birds. Combined with short stout legs, this has helped the kiwi become internationally well known.

quiz_question: DNA shows kiwis are more closely related to which extinct giant bird than to New Zealand's own moa?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, DNA sequence comparisons have yielded the conclusion that kiwi are much more closely related to the extinct Malagasy elephant birds than to the moa with which they shared New Zealand. This suggests the kiwi lineage flew to New Zealand rather than walking there with the moa.

quiz_question: What distinctive physical feature makes cockatoos unique among parrots?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cockatoos are recognisable by their prominent crests and curved bills. Their plumage is generally less colourful than other parrots, being mainly white, grey, or black, but the distinctive crest immediately identifies them.

quiz_question: Where do cockatoos exclusively nest, making tree clearing a major survival threat?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cockatoos are monogamous and nest in tree hollows. Some species have been adversely affected by habitat loss, particularly from a shortage of suitable nesting hollows after large, mature trees are cleared.

quiz_question: From where on their bodies do male birds-of-paradise grow elaborate display feathers?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, male birds-of-paradise tend to have very long, elaborate feathers extending from the beak, wings, tail, or head. The family has 44 species in 17 genera, found mainly in New Guinea and eastern Australia.

quiz_question: How many species are in the birds-of-paradise family Paradisaeidae?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the birds-of-paradise family (Paradisaeidae) has 44 species in 17 genera, found mainly in New Guinea, as well as eastern Australia and the Moluccas. They are confined mostly to dense rainforest habitats.

quiz_question: What is the world's third most popular pet, after dogs and cats?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, budgerigars are the third most popular pet in the world, after the domesticated dog and cat. First recorded in 1805, they are popular due to their small size, low cost, and ability to mimic human speech.

quiz_question: What unusual breeding strategy do wild budgerigars use instead of fixed territories?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, budgerigars have survived harsh inland conditions for over five million years. Their success can be attributed to a nomadic lifestyle and their ability to breed while on the move — adapting opportunistically rather than defending fixed territories.

quiz_question: How does the secretarybird primarily kill its prey?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the secretarybird hunts and catches prey on the ground, often stomping on victims to kill them. It feeds on insects and small vertebrates. Its crane-like legs give it a height of up to 1.3 m.

quiz_question: Which African bird of prey appears on two national coats of arms?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the secretarybird appears on the coats of arms of Sudan and South Africa. It is classified as Endangered by the IUCN, its population declining from habitat destruction.

quiz_question: What is the IUCN conservation status of the secretarybird?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, the secretarybird's total population is experiencing a rapid decline, probably as a result of habitat destruction. The species is therefore classed as Endangered by the IUCN.

quiz_question: Which birds uniquely have their first two neck vertebrae fused?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, hornbills are the only birds in which the atlas and axis (first and second neck vertebrae) are fused together; this probably provides a more stable platform for carrying their large, colorful bills.

quiz_question: Hornbills and toucans look alike but are not closely related. What is this called?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the Neotropical realm, toucans occupy the hornbills' ecological niche — an example of convergent evolution. Despite their close appearances, the two groups are not very closely related, with toucans allied with woodpeckers while hornbills are allied with hoopoes.

quiz_question: Where do toucans typically build their nests?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, toucans make their nests in tree hollows and holes excavated by other animals such as woodpeckers. Their large colorful bill has very limited use as an excavation tool.

quiz_question: Why do scientists think toucans fence with their bills and wrestle?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, toucans sometimes fence with their bills and wrestle, which scientists hypothesize they do to establish dominance hierarchies. They are usually found in pairs or small flocks.

quiz_question: What did genetic evidence reveal about the shoebill's true relatives?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the shoebill has a somewhat stork-like overall form and was previously classified as a stork in the order Ciconiiformes, but genetic evidence places it with pelicans and herons in the Pelecaniformes.

quiz_question: What unusual object shape is the shoebill's bill compared to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the shoebill is named for its enormous, shoe-shaped bill. It is also known as the whale-headed stork, whalebill, and shoe-billed stork. It lives in tropical East Africa in large swamps.

quiz_question: Which tern species is critically endangered?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, terns are relatively free from natural predators, but most species are declining due directly or indirectly to human activities including habitat loss, pollution, disturbance, and predation by introduced mammals. The Chinese crested tern is critically endangered.

quiz_question: What distinctive tail shape characterizes most terns?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, terns are slender, lightly built birds with long, forked tails, narrow wings, long bills, and relatively short legs. They have a worldwide distribution and are normally found near the sea, rivers, or wetlands.

quiz_question: In Accipiter hawks, which sex tends to be larger?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the genus Accipiter, females tend to be larger than males. Accipiter hawks often ambush prey, mainly small birds and mammals, after a short chase.

quiz_question: How do Accipiter hawks typically capture their prey?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Accipiter hawks often ambush their prey, mainly small birds and mammals, capturing them after a short chase. They have short, broad, rounded wings and a long tail which helps them maneuver in flight through wooded or shrubby areas.

quiz_question: In height, where do rheas rank among living ratites?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, rheas are distantly related to the two African ostriches and Australia's emu (the largest, second-largest and third-largest living ratites, respectively), with rheas placing just behind the emu in height and overall size.

quiz_question: Which South American flightless bird has a feral population established in Germany?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, a feral population of the greater rhea in Germany appears to be growing, and control efforts are underway to manage the birds' population growth. The greater rhea is native to South America.

quiz_question: How many bird species are listed among the 100 World's Worst Invasive Species?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 2000 the IUCN Species Survival Commission declared the common myna one of the world's most invasive species and one of only three birds listed among '100 of the World's Worst Invasive Species' that pose a threat to biodiversity, agriculture and human interests.

quiz_question: What environment has the common myna adapted especially well to?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common myna is an omnivorous open woodland bird with a strong territorial instinct that has adapted extremely well to urban environments. Its range is increasing at such a rapid rate that it was declared one of the world's most invasive species in 2000.

quiz_question: What unusual feature do hoatzin chicks have on their wings?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the hoatzin is notable for its chicks having primitive claws on two of their wing digits. This gives the hoatzin a prehistoric appearance and connects it to ancient bird lineages.

quiz_question: Which bird is unique for fermenting plant matter in its digestive system like a cow?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the hoatzin is unique among birds in possessing a digestive system that significantly supports the fermentation and effective breakdown of plant matter, a trait more commonly known from herbivorous ungulate-ruminant mammals and some primates.

quiz_question: What is the local name for Guyana's national bird, the hoatzin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the hoatzin is the national bird of Guyana, where the local name for this bird is Canje pheasant. It lives in swamps, riparian forests, and mangroves of the Amazon and Orinoco Basins.

quiz_question: What is the correct term for the female of the species commonly called a 'peacock'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, male peafowl are referred to as peacocks, and female peafowl are referred to as peahens. Despite this, 'peacock' is usually used to refer to both sexes in common language.

quiz_question: Who first proposed that peacock tail plumage evolved through sexual selection?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Charles Darwin suggested that the peacock's elaborate iridescent coloration and large train served to attract females, and that these showy features had evolved by sexual selection. More recently, Amotz Zahavi proposed the handicap principle as an alternate explanation.

quiz_question: Which peafowl species is native only to the Congo Basin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, there are three peafowl species: the blue or Indian peafowl originally from the Indian subcontinent, the green peafowl from Southeast Asia, and the Congo peafowl, which is native only to the Congo Basin.

quiz_question: What is the maximum length a Komodo dragon can reach?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Komodo dragon is the largest living lizard, growing up to 3 metres (10 ft) in length and weighing up to 150 kg (330 lb). It is endemic to the Indonesian islands of Komodo, Rinca, Flores, and Gili Motang.

quiz_question: At what speed can a Komodo dragon sprint?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Komodo dragons can run at up to 20 km/h (12 mph) in short bursts and are also capable swimmers, enabling them to move between islands. They live up to 30 years in the wild.

quiz_question: How do young Komodo dragons protect themselves from cannibalistic adults?
correct_answer: Live in trees and cover themselves in feces
explanation: According to Wikipedia, young Komodo dragons are vulnerable to cannibalism by adults and protect themselves by living in trees and rolling in feces, making themselves unappealing to eat. Adults generally avoid eating feces-covered prey.

quiz_question: The tuatara is the sole surviving member of which reptile order?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tuatara (Sphenodon punctatus) is the only surviving member of the order Rhynchocephalia, whose other members became extinct in the Cretaceous period. Its lineage diverged from other reptiles approximately 240 million years ago, making it a true 'living fossil.'

quiz_question: Approximately how many base pairs are in the tuatara's genome?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the tuatara genome is estimated at 5 to 6 billion base pairs — one of the largest reptile genomes known and comparable in size to the human genome. Its ancient lineage makes it valuable to evolutionary biology.

quiz_question: In what year did New Zealand first legally protect the tuatara?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tuatara have been protected by law in New Zealand since 1895. They are now found mainly on predator-free offshore islands and are considered a taonga (treasured species) by the Maori people.

quiz_question: How do painted turtle hatchlings survive winter temperatures?
correct_answer: They can survive being frozen solid
explanation: According to Wikipedia, painted turtles survive winter by supercooling their blood to just below freezing. Hatchlings that overwinter in their nest can survive body temperatures as low as -10°C, essentially freezing solid without cell damage — a feat unique among reptiles.

quiz_question: What remarkable aging property do painted turtles exhibit?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, painted turtles exhibit negligible senescence — meaning their organ function does not decline with age and their mortality rate does not increase with age. They can live more than 55 years in the wild and their fossil record dates back 15 million years.

quiz_question: Which species is one of only two venomous lizards native to the United States?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Gila monster (Heloderma suspectum) is one of only two venomous lizards native to the United States, the other being the closely related Mexican beaded lizard. It is one of the largest lizards in North America. Its venom glands are modified salivary glands in the lower jaw.
